content:
Search is no longer ten options to compare — and the career path for marketers is wider than “SEO or obsolete.”

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Masanao Tomomatsu on UnsplashI learned marketing in the blue links era.
You typed a query. You got ten options. The job was to climb — better content, better links, better technical health, until your URL moved up. The buyer did the comparison. You influenced the list.
That mental model shaped my entire career. Meetings about position. Reports about position. Identity tied to position.
Then I watched a buyer ask ChatGPT a question and accept the first synthesized answer without opening a single tab. No scrolling. No comparison shopping across ten sites. One answer. One recommendation. Done.
The ten blue links are not gone everywhere yet. But they are no longer the center of gravity. And if the surface changes, the career path has to change with it — not disappear, but widen.
The skill that aged and the skill that didn’t
Climbing lists is a real skill. I am not dismissing it. Keyword research, SERP analysis, link building, technical audits — these still matter.
What aged is treating that skill as the whole job.
The new surface rewards a different primary skill: becoming the source an AI chooses when it builds a single answer. That is not the same as ranking first. It is overlap, not identity.
When people ask whether SEO jobs will die, I think they are asking the wrong question. The SEO who refuses to learn AI search is in trouble. The marketer who understands discoverability — for crawlers and for models — is more valuable than before.
I am not an SEO Executive. I am a growth marketer with an engineering degree and an MBA. But I had to unlearn “my job is to win the list” and learn “my job is to win the answer.” That reframe opened a career path I did not see coming.
The career path that is wider than SEO
Here is what I tell marketers who are anxious about titles and lanes:
The old lane: SEO specialist, list climber, traffic owner.
The expanding lane: Visibility strategist — someone who makes brands and people findable, citable, and accurately described across search surfaces that no longer look like one results page.
That lane includes SEO skills. It also includes positioning, entity thinking, prompt-based research, answer-first writing, and the ability to explain technical gaps to non-technical stakeholders.
You do not have to rebrand on LinkedIn tomorrow. You do have to expand what you are learning before your job description expands without you.
What I stopped waiting for
I stopped waiting for my company to create an “AEO role” before I learned AEO.
I stopped assuming SEO teams would own this and I could stay in my lane.
I stopped treating AI search as a niche topic for early adopters. Buyers are already there. Careers lag buyers by about eighteen months. That gap is opportunity if you move now.
A three-step career pivot (without quitting)
Step 1: Audit your transferable skills. Technical literacy, content quality, authority building — if you have done SEO or growth work, you have muscles. Name them. Do not start from zero.
Step 2: Add one AI-search habit. Weekly prompt test. Monthly citation log. Fifteen minutes. Consistency beats intensity.
Step 3: Publish one piece of learning in public. Not a hot take. One observation from your audit. “I asked AI who to hire in my field. Here is what it said.” That post is a career signal — you are paying attention before it is mandatory.
I did step three reluctantly. It attracted more inbound than six months of generic content calendar work.
The emotional part nobody maps on a career ladder
There is grief in this transition. I liked the old game. It was legible. You could see position four move to three.
The new game is fuzzier. You might be influential without a click. You might be named in an answer you cannot fully attribute. That feels like loss if you need the dashboard to validate your worth.
I had to separate “I am valuable” from “I am easily counted.” That is personal growth dressed as industry change.
For marketers who are not SEOs
This is the part I wish someone had said to me earlier: you do not need to become an SEO to stay relevant. You need to understand what SEOs know about discoverability and pair it with what growth marketers know about positioning and pipeline.
The career moat is breadth with depth — able to speak technical, strategic, and buyer language in one conversation.
The ten blue links era rewarded specialization in climbing. The answer era rewards people who can make clarity happen across the whole stack.
What this looks like on a Tuesday
Practically, my Tuesdays now include a visibility block that would have confused me two years ago. Fifteen minutes of prompt tests. Ten minutes updating a one-page “what I do” doc that feeds every bio. No keyword tool open. No rank tracker refresh.
The first month felt inefficient. The sixth month produced a speaking invite from someone who found me through an AI answer, not a Google result. That single data point did more for my career confidence than a quarter of green traffic arrows.
If you are employed and worried about scope creep, frame the habit as competitive research — because that is what it is. You are studying where buyer attention forms before it reaches any funnel you currently measure.
Reading the room without panic
I talk to SEO friends regularly. Some are energized. Some are scared. The scared ones usually think the job is vanishing. The energized ones already added “visibility” to how they describe their work — same skills, wider surface.
You do not have to love the shift to navigate it. You do have to name it accurately. “Search is changing” is too vague to act on. “Buyers get one synthesized answer instead of ten links” is a picture you can plan against.
I keep a short list of practitioners whose careers I admire in this transition. When I feel behind, I read their bios and notice how they phrase expertise now. Less “I rank pages.” More “I make brands discoverable where buyers decide.” Patterns emerge. That list is cheaper than a course and more honest than a panic scroll.
The takeaway
SEO jobs are not disappearing. List-climbing as the only career game is.
I am building a different career now — not by abandoning what I learned in the blue links era, but by stacking new skills on top of it. The marketers who do that will not ask whether their title still exists in five years. They will be the ones defining what comes next.

If this helped, follow me here on Medium — I write about AI Search Visibility Strategies, personal growth and productivity for marketers navigating the AI era.
